title: Marketplace Trust Asymmetry

content.statement: One bad experience damages more than many good ones build.
content.question: What's the experience in your business where a single bad event could undo 50 good ones? How do you bulletproof it?
sop: Identify the single highest-stakes moment in your customer experience (payment, delivery, safety, first interaction).
Estimate the Negative Impact of a bad version of that moment, in lost customers, reviews, or reputation (see calculation, page 5).
Estimate the Positive Impact of a good version of that same moment (see calculation, page 5).
Divide Negative Impact by Positive Impact to see how asymmetric that moment really is (see calculation, page 5).
Put your strongest quality control, backup plan, or double-check specifically on whichever moment scores highest.
content.explainer: Trust builds linearly and breaks exponentially. Protect the weakest moment fanatically.
questions: What's the experience in your business where a single bad event could undo 50 good ones? How do you bulletproof it?
How many perfect transactions does it take to undo the damage of one bad review, and have you ever actually tested that?
Is your worst possible customer experience something you've designed around, or something you're hoping never happens?
content.equation_example: Negative Impact estimated at 5 lost customers per bad incident, Positive Impact estimated at 0.1 customers gained per good experience. 5 divided by 0.1 equals 50. Higher means that moment is dangerously asymmetric, one failure erases many successes. Lower, closer to 1, means good and bad experiences carry roughly equal weight, a more forgiving part of your business.
content.closing_statement: One bad moment can undo dozens of good ones.

caption:
Principle Nº 78: Marketplace Trust Asymmetry

One bad experience damages more than many good ones build. Trust builds linearly and breaks exponentially.

Ask yourself: What's the experience in your business where a single bad event could undo 50 good ones? How do you bulletproof it?
